chunk_summary: This section discusses diagnostic strategies for inflammatory diarrhea when fecal calprotectin is unavailable, emphasizing objective risk assessment, nutritional outcomes, and differential diagnosis with intestinal TB, particularly in India.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of objective risk assessment in diagnosing inflammatory diarrhea without fecal calprotectin.
Identify key clinical and laboratory markers for high-probability inflammatory disease.
Differentiate between Crohn’s disease and intestinal TB using epidemiologic, clinical, and diagnostic criteria.
Recognize the role of nutrition support and TB screening in managing inflammatory bowel disease.
key_facts: Blood in stool, nocturnal symptoms, weight loss, delayed puberty, iron deficiency anemia, or low serum albumin indicate high probability of inflammatory disease.
Basic inflammatory labs include CBC, CRP ± ESR, and growth Z scores/velocity documentation.
In India, intestinal TB is a critical differential diagnosis for Crohn’s disease; both conditions can present with low albumin and high inflammatory burden.
Endoscopic evaluation is prioritized over prolonged dietary restriction trials in high-risk cases.
Baseline TB screening is mandatory before biologic escalation in confirmed IBD to prevent catastrophic outcomes under anti-TNF therapy.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: In India, intestinal TB is a key alternative diagnosis to Crohn’s disease and both under- and over-treatment worsen nutrition.
document_key_topics: Differentiating Crohn’s disease and intestinal TB
TB screening in inflammatory bowel disease
